title: Modernising the census | Stats NZ
content:
## Modernising the census
Changes to modernise our census have been announced. Learn what they are, and why they are happening.

## Census data is important to us all
It provides an official count of people and dwellings in Aotearoa New Zealand.
It gives a snapshot of life, people, and communities to guide planning and decision-making.
It helps us understand who we are and what we need.
Changes to modernise our census have been announced. In the future, Aotearoa New Zealand’s census data will be produced using more of the information already collected by government (known as administrative or admin data).

## The census is changing
Stats NZ and the Minister of Statistics have announced that Aotearoa New Zealand’s census is set to be modernised.
We are moving to a new way of producing census data, by increasing the use of information already collected by government (known as administrative or admin data). Surveys will continue to play an important role, to help confirm the accuracy of the admin data and collect more information.
The new approach builds on the ‘combined models’ used since 2018. Those models prioritised information collected through the census survey, supplemented by admin data.
As part of our work to modernise the census, we will also work closely with iwi Māori and smaller population groups to develop partnership-based design solutions, like targeted surveys, to help meet their data needs.
Why modernise the census describes the reasons why Stats NZ has been working to create a sustainable, modern census for the future.
Cabinet papers and key documents shares more information on the government’s decision-making process to modernise the census since 2012, and other information that has supported and informed our work, including decision papers, reports, and notable research and methodology papers.

### Introducing the changes gradually
The changes to modernise the census are significant. To help ensure they are introduced as smoothly as possible, we have built more time into the process.
New Zealand’s next census will now take place in 2030 (subject to legislation amendments).
The admin data-first approach to the census will be introduced in stages and strengthened over the next five to 10 years. Below is an indicative timeline:
Census survey: field test
Public consultation: census content and collection approach
November to December 2025
Census content decisions published
Census high-level design published
Amendments to Data and Statistics Act 2022 confirmed
Data release: transitional population measures (admin data only)
Census survey: design and readiness test
February to April 2027
October 2027 to June 2028
Data release: transitional population measures (admin data only)
July 2028 to June 2029
Data release: official population and dwelling measures (admin data only)
July 2029 to June 2030
Data release: official population and dwelling measures (admin data only)
Te Kupenga | Māori wellbeing survey
Te Kupenga | Māori wellbeing survey
Data release: official census data and statistics (admin and survey data) Data made available to the Representation Commission for boundary review Annual releases from now on
Data release: official census data and statistics (admin and survey data)
Data made available to the Representation Commission for boundary review
Annual releases from now on
From late 2030 or early 2031
Independent review of the census
There’s a lot to do to prepare for the next census. Now that the Government has endorsed the introduction of an admin data-first census, we will progress with the detailed plans to move from design into actioning the new approach.
Stats NZ will work with other government agencies and crown entities to make the necessary changes and improvements to New Zealand’s admin data system.
Some proposed legislative changes are also underway:
- The Data and Statistics Act 2022 will be updated through an amendment Bill (Census Bill) to reflect the change in census approach and timing. Visit First reading of Data and Statistics (Census) Amendment Bill for more information about these changes.
- Related amendments to the Electoral Act 1993 are also being considered by Parliament. The Parliament website has more information.
- The Census Bill has been introduced and had its first reading in Parliament. Visit the Parliament website to follow the Census Bill’s progress.
We will also continue working with partners, customers and priority communities, to ensure our work to modernise the census is informed by the data needs of communities and organisations throughout New Zealand.
As we progress the work to modernise the census, we’ll provide regular updates to our customers and data users, including on our website and through our newsletters .

### The process for this change
Modernising the census: the process for change has information about why the census is changing, and the decision-making process.

## What the next census will look like
When the census began in 1851, surveying was the only way to get information about most of the population. Today, data is easier to source from other places than it used to be. In recent years surveying has been supplemented with administrative or admin data.
Admin data is information that other organisations, such as government departments, collect from you as part of their day-to-day work. This includes the kind of data organisations might collect to deliver health and education services, record events such as births, deaths, and marriages, or records of transactions such as tax records.

#### In many cases, Stats NZ is already using admin data
For example, we use admin data to produce population estimates and projections, and statistics about international migration, household income, and child poverty.
Admin data provides us with the opportunity to get a clear picture of New Zealand, people, dwellings, households, communities, and families without having to always ask every question in surveys like census. This also saves you having to provide certain information again and again.
Survey and admin data have co-existed for a long time. In fact, the Census and Statistics Office talked about them a century ago in the 1924 New Zealand Official Yearbook.
Today, within a modern evolving society, changing needs, changing technology, and changing expectations, there are more opportunities and reasons for us to mix the two sources of data together. For example, we could:
- combine data from the Ministry of Education about tertiary education enrolment with survey data about people’s study status
- combine data from Inland Revenue about tax with people’s survey responses about their income.
Since 2018, we’ve been using a combination of survey and admin data to produce census data. When responses have not been given, or lacked detail in census forms, survey data has been merged with alternative data sources (including admin data and data from earlier censuses) to help provide complete information. This process is referred to as a combined model.
The combined model was introduced in the 2018 Census due to lower-than-expected response rates. Using a combined census model for the 2023 Census sets out how Stats NZ successfully used a combined model by design to produce the highest quality data possible for the 2023 Census.
We are now ready to move toward a new way of producing census data and statistics.
Our next census will still be conducted using a combined approach. The difference is that we will be prioritising the use of admin data. This means we are moving from using admin data to help support census survey information, to an approach that uses admin data-first to produce New Zealand’s census data.
As part of our admin data-first approach, surveys will continue to play an important role. Survey responses will help confirm the accuracy of the admin data we collect and to gather more information. A new annual sample survey of the population will be introduced and this, together with the use of modern statistical techniques, will all be combined to produce New Zealand’s official census data and statistics.
As part of the new approach, we are committed to working closely with iwi Māori and smaller population groups to develop partnership-based design solutions, like targeted surveys, to help meet their data needs.
International examples of census models show a trend away from traditional survey-led census models towards approaches that incorporate greater use of admin data. Some countries already use a combined admin data-first model, while others have been running an admin data-only census for many years.
Using more admin data, supported by survey data, gives us the opportunity to:
- expand on the statistics we publish, as we will be able to access data from a wider range of sources
- remove questions from the census where we already have, or can source, that information from admin data
- use the census survey to collect new information that is meaningful and important to the people of Aotearoa New Zealand
- save people time and effort by not asking them for information that is collected in other ways
- provide more timely data because admin data is collected continuously
- respond to new and emerging data needs because administrative data is already available
- develop tailored collection approaches to meet specific data needs.
If you have any questions about our work to modernise the census, or you'd like to speak with someone about engaging with your organisation or network of community members, please contact us:
Freephone: 0508 525 525 Email: info@stats.govt.nz

## The value of data for communities
Census data is important to us all.
Why the census matters tells the stories of communities around Aotearoa New Zealand who benefit from the kind of data that is gathered in the census.
Ensuring the data and statistics we produce are useful to people and communities is the key driver for change. This is why we have made a commitment to ongoing engagement with a wide range of community and sector representatives to meaningfully shape the future of census in Aotearoa New Zealand.
One of the ways we are doing this is by creating (or formalising) new community design groups to help shape the next census, and other key statistics programmes. The groups will provide a regular, structured opportunity for communities to directly contribute to our design and planning work.

## Data helps people access and use New Zealand Sign Language

## Data helps connect rangatahi to job opportunities

## Data helps Whānau Āwhina Plunket support children and their families

## Data helps meet our community health needs

## Agricultural snapshot: Insights into farmers’ lives
Farmers across New Zealand will soon participate in the 2026 Agricultural Production Survey. This article uses statistics drawn from several Stats NZ datasets to provide insights into trends in farmers’ employment, wellbeing, income, and other measures.

## Better data for ethnic communities
The data needs of Ethnic Communities are the focus of a new relationship agreement between Stats NZ and the Ministry for Ethnic Communities.

## Communities are an essential voice in New Zealand’s data system
The creation of Pacific, rainbow, and homeless community advisory groups marks a significant step in Stats NZ’s commitment to a modern, inclusive census.

## Working with communities to design the next census
A newly formed Crown-Māori Statistical Design Forum met last month for its inaugural hui in Wellington. The forum is one of several collaborative design groups being established by Stats NZ to help ensure that the data needs and perspectives of communities are included in the design of the census.

### Subscribe to Census News
The Census News is a regular email newsletter with information about the census. It includes the latest information and developments about the census, and feedback opportunities.
